title: Should the Plural Elohim of Psalm 82 Be Understood as Men or Divine Beings? (ETS 2010)
author: heiser

entries.title: Elohim as a 'Place of Residence' Term — The Key to Psalm 82
entries.content_summary: Five different entities are referenced as elohim in the Hebrew Bible: (A) Yahweh (over 2000 times); (B) the elohim of Yahweh's heavenly council (Psalm 82, 89; Deut 32:8-9,43); (C) gods of foreign nations (1 Kings 11:33); (D) demons (Deut 32:17); (E) disembodied human dead (1 Sam 28:13); (F) angels (Gen 35:7). This variety means elohim is NOT identified with one particular set of attributes. It is a 'place of residence' term — it identifies the proper domain of the entity. All figures called elohim inhabit the non-human realm. Within the spiritual world there is ontological differentiation, rank, and power: Yahweh is an elohim, but no other elohim is Yahweh. The modern term 'monotheism' (17th century) is deficient for describing this, since it carries mental baggage of identifying 'god' with a single set of attributes held only by Yahweh.
entries.content_detail: Elohim as a term does not refer to a set of attributes or ontology. Those issues are not explicated by a term; they are described in the Hebrew Bible. The biblical writers would not equate Yahweh in an ontological or qualitative sense with demons, angels, the dead, or other gods. We have nothing to fear from letting the text say what it says.

entries.title: Psalm 89:5-7 Rules Out Human Judges — The Council Is 'In the Clouds'
entries.chapter_or_episode: Section 3.1: Psalm 82 Should Not Be Isolated
entries.content_summary: Psalm 89:5-7 eliminates any ambiguity about whether the divine council elohim are human: 'Who in the CLOUDS (basshahaq) can be compared to Yahweh? Who among the sons of God (bene elim) is like Yahweh, a God greatly feared in the council (sod) of the holy ones?' Psalm 89 explicitly places the council 'in the clouds.' There is no text in the entirety of the Hebrew Bible that says or suggests there is a group of human judges in the heavens ruling with Yahweh over the nations. That position is offered only because of a perceived threat to monotheism, not because it has any textual merit.
entries.content_detail: Rather than isolate Psalm 82, we should ask whether there are other passages with the same language of divine plurality in a council that rule out equating that language with human beings. Psalm 89:5-7 is exactly such a passage.

entries.title: Deuteronomy 32 as the Backdrop to Psalm 82 — The Judgment of the Gods of the Nations
entries.content_summary: The council elohim in Psalm 82 are being judged for corrupt administration. The last verse implores God to 'inherit' (nahal) all the nations — the same lemma used for Yahweh's punitive judgment at Babel when He allotted the nations to the sons of God (Deut 32:8-9) and allotted those divine beings to the nations He was disinheriting (Deut 4:19-20). The psalmist is asking Yahweh to reverse the Babel judgment — to take back the nations from the corrupt divine beings. This is the cosmic geography worldview: each nation was under the dominion of a divine being assigned by Yahweh. These beings failed in their governance, and Psalm 82 records their sentencing. Psalm 82:8 is an eschatological cry for Yahweh to reclaim all nations — fulfilled in the Great Commission and Pentecost.
entries.content_detail: The parallel between Psalm 82:8 (nahal — 'inherit' the nations) and Deuteronomy 32:8 (nahal — 'gave the nations their inheritance') creates a direct theological link. The nations were given as an inheritance to the sons of God at Babel; Psalm 82 pronounces judgment on those divine stewards; and the psalmist calls for Yahweh to take the inheritance back. No Israelite elder ever had jurisdiction over all the nations, but the divine beings of Deut 32:8-9 did.
